questions.q: In a statically typed language with type inference, when you write `let x = 5`, how does the compiler determine x's type?
options: By asking the programmer at compile time
By inferring from the literal value and subsequent usage in the code
By deferring the type check until runtime
By assigning a universal type that works with any operation
questions.explanation: Type inference systems like those in ML and Haskell examine literal values and how variables are used in expressions to automatically deduce their type. The compiler determines x is an integer without explicit annotation. Static typing with inference combines compile-time checking with reduced annotation overhead.

questions.q: What is the key difference between strong and weak type systems?
options: Strong types prevent all runtime errors; weak types are not compiled
Strong types enforce strict compatibility between types; weak types allow implicit conversions between incompatible types
Strong types are faster; weak types are slower
Strong types only exist in object-oriented languages
questions.explanation: A strong type system (like Python or Java) requires explicit type compatibility and resists implicit conversions between unrelated types. Weak systems (like C) allow implicit conversions between arbitrary types (e.g., char to int), risking unintended behavior. Both can be compiled or interpreted.

questions.q: What does parametric polymorphism enable in languages like Haskell and Java?
options: Functions to have different implementations for each specific type at compile time only
Generic code to work with multiple types while maintaining type safety
All functions to accept any type and runtime checks handle correctness
Operators to behave differently based on the types of their operands
questions.explanation: Parametric polymorphism (generics) lets you write functions like `map :: (a → b) → [a] → [b]` that work for any types a and b while the type system still enforces correctness. Java generics and Haskell's type variables exemplify this, ensuring type safety without rewriting code for each type.

questions.q: In type theory, what is variance, and why does contravariance matter for function types?
options: Variance describes how type parameters vary with subtyping; function parameter types are contravariant to preserve type safety
Variance is a synonym for polymorphism; contravariance makes code run faster
Variance measures performance overhead; contravariance eliminates boxing costs
Variance only applies to collection types, not function types
questions.explanation: Covariance allows a subtype to be used where a supertype is expected; contravariance does the reverse for inputs. Function parameter types are contravariant: if Cat ⊆ Animal, a function expecting Animal can safely receive (Animal → Result), but not (Cat → Result). This ensures type safety.

questions.q: Why do languages like TypeScript and Python use gradual typing?
options: It allows developers to mix dynamically typed and statically typed code in the same program
Gradual typing is slower but more flexible than static typing
It eliminates the need for runtime type checks entirely
Gradual typing only works with interpreted languages
questions.explanation: Gradual typing permits portions of code to be statically type-checked while others remain dynamically typed. TypeScript transpiles to JavaScript, allowing incremental adoption of type annotations. Python's type hints are similar—code works without them but tools can catch errors. This bridges static and dynamic worlds.

questions.q: What does the 'Liskov Substitution Principle' require in terms of type system behavior?
options: Subtypes must be usable wherever their supertypes are expected without breaking correctness
All types must inherit from a single root type object
Type checking must occur at runtime for all operations
Functions must have only one return type across all code paths
questions.explanation: LSP states that subtype instances must be substitutable for supertype instances without violating correctness. A subtypes type system must ensure contravariance for inputs and covariance for outputs. Violating LSP (e.g., Dog.bark() throwing Exception when Animal.speak() doesn't) breaks substitutability.

questions.q: In dependent type systems like Idris, what do dependent types enable that standard type systems cannot?
options: Proof that specific properties hold at compile time, not just type shape verification
Types that depend on runtime values, which are checked after execution completes
Elimination of pattern matching in all cases
Automatic parallelization of type-checked code
questions.explanation: Dependent types can encode logical propositions directly in types. In Idris, `Vector n a` is a list of exactly n elements; the type depends on the value n. This allows proofs that code is correct—not just that it doesn't crash. Standard types only verify shape and compatibility, not properties.

questions.q: What problem does row polymorphism solve in type systems?
options: It allows functions to work with records/objects differing in field content while enforcing type safety
It eliminates the need for inheritance hierarchies entirely
It prevents records from having any common fields
It only works when all record types are defined before compilation
questions.explanation: Row polymorphism lets a function operate on any record containing at least certain fields, ignoring extra fields. A function expecting {name:String, age:Int, ...r} works with {name:String, age:Int, email:String} without knowing email exists. This enables flexible duck-typing with static guarantees.

questions.q: What is the difference between nominal and structural typing?
options: Nominal typing requires explicit type declarations; structural typing infers types automatically
Nominal typing checks type names match; structural typing checks shapes (fields/methods) match
Nominal typing is only used in dynamically typed languages
Structural typing is faster because it skips name lookups
questions.explanation: Go uses structural typing: any struct with matching methods/fields satisfies an interface, regardless of declared type. Java uses nominal typing: a class must explicitly implement an interface. Nominal is stricter (you intend the relationship), structural is more flexible (shape is what matters).

questions.q: Why can a language be both dynamically typed and strongly typed?
options: Dynamic typing refers to when types are checked; strong typing refers to how permissive implicit conversions are allowed
Dynamic typing and strong typing are mutually exclusive by definition
It is impossible; all strongly typed languages must be statically typed
Dynamic languages cannot enforce type safety at any point
questions.explanation: Python is dynamically typed (runtime type checks) yet strongly typed (no implicit int-to-string conversion). Ruby is similar. Java is statically typed and strong. C is statically typed but weak. The axes are independent: when types are checked (static vs dynamic) versus how permissive conversion rules are (strong vs weak).
